data.title: The biggest highlight of Musk's version of WeChat has nothing to do with WeChat itself.
data.description:
Only three days remain until Musk's "super app dream" comes true. On April 17th, XChat is expected to officially launch on the Apple App Store, with simultaneous global downloads.

This is definitely not an ordinary app launch. On Musk's crazy and massive business strategy chessboard, XChat is another core and most indispensable move after his $44 billion acquisition of Twitter and its violent renaming to X.

His anticipation for this day dates back to 2022. After acquiring Twitter, Musk mentioned WeChat in almost every public appearance. He said, "In China, you basically live in WeChat because it's so useful for daily life. If we can achieve that on X, even just get close, it will be a huge success." Musk isn't obsessed with WeChat's chat interface, but with its status as a digital life operating system—payments, communication, ride-hailing, food delivery, utility bills, all in one app. If acquiring Twitter was his ticket to the super app gamble, then XChat is the first card he's played. Despite being touted as Musk's version of WeChat, XChat has become more like Telegram? Functionally, XChat focuses on privacy-first, standalone chat application. Registration does not require a phone number; simply log in with your X account. Messages support self-destructing messages, recall, and editing. Group chats can accommodate up to 481 people, and file transfers have a maximum limit of 4GB. Cross-device audio and video calls are all built-in. Download requires iOS 26.0 or later. The application-level prohibition of screenshots and screen recording attempts to plug the loopholes for content leakage at the source. This may be a favorite feature of some tech industry bosses. Grok AI is directly embedded in the chat interface and can be called up at any time in the conversation for summarizing content, real-time translation, or planning itineraries. XChat's overall positioning follows a clean, private, and minimally disruptive approach. The interface is stripped of the main X app's feed, ads, and trending topics, leaving dedicated space for private conversations. It initially supports 46 languages, including Simplified and Traditional Chinese. Bearing Musk's distinct personal imprint, XChat not only makes bold changes to its positioning but also progresses at an astonishing speed, even revealing a degree of radicalism and fervor. Last June, Musk publicly teased X; by December, X employee Nikita Bier was already publicly endorsing it, marveling at how the team "completed the encrypted private messaging migration in just three months," and taking a jab at competitors: "Facebook took three years to do this." This March, the iOS version of TestFlight opened for testing, initially with 1000 participants, quickly expanding to... The 5,000 slots were snapped up within two hours of the announcement, but the high level of attention was accompanied by extremely polarized reviews. @Nicole_yang88, a user who received her TestFlight qualification in March, wrote: "The overall smoothness is extremely high, with almost no lag. The interface follows a minimalist approach, with clear hierarchy and restrained color scheme, and it does indeed resemble the clean style of iMessage." She also specifically mentioned the seamless integration of one-click authorization login with the main X app and account data, "without any sense of disconnect when switching apps."

But some people don't buy it at all. Test user @ohxiyu posted: "Upon opening it, it's pixel-perfectly identical to X's private messages. So why separate it? Private messages, requests, and harassment are all mixed together, no different from current DMs. Want to chat with someone? There's no contact list; you can only search through chat history." Even more baffling is the design of the private mode. If the other party has enabled self-destructing messages, you receive no notification, and the content disappears after a short while. He said, "At least Telegram pops up a notification. There's not even a menu. It feels like they just put a DM page in a shell and threw it out." Even before XChat was officially available for download, trouble had already begun. On April 11th, the day pre-orders opened, users issued warnings: a Russian version of XChat had appeared on the App Store, with an icon and name highly similar to the real product. After downloading, it would require users to provide credit card information and ID to prove their age. The image on the right is the official version and a safe download. Currently, the only reliable path is through the official Apple App Store (overseas version), ensuring the developer is X Corp. Blogger @Imlaomao personally experienced this issue: "After accidentally entering my credit card information, I realized something was wrong and immediately canceled all my credit cards." While he stated there was no direct evidence to prove the app definitely had a problem, he advised everyone to "prioritize safety and be cautious." Is the so-called "Bitcoin-style encryption" just a word game? In all of XChat's promotional rhetoric, "Bitcoin-style encryption" is undoubtedly the most eye-catching phrase. Musk, a master of traffic generation, successfully used this somewhat geeky term to conjure up a cyberpunk-like image in countless people's minds: chat logs on the blockchain, decentralized storage. The ideal is often far from reality. According to Nvidia security developer Matthew Garrett's technical analysis of early versions of XChat, XChat's message encryption layer uses libsodium's box encryption scheme. This scheme itself has undergone extensive auditing and can be considered robust. However, Musk didn't clarify one point: libsodium's core is written in C, and X uses the C version, not the "completely new Rust architecture" he claimed. Regarding key management, XChat uses the open-source protocol Juicebox—this protocol has its own white paper and wasn't developed by X itself. Its design involves encrypting your private key, splitting it into fragments, and storing them on multiple servers controlled by X. When you switch to a new device, you enter a 4-digit PIN code, the system retrieves the fragments from the servers, reassembles the key, and restores all chat history. The problem is that X's three backend domains are all under x.com, presumably directly controlled by Twitter. The Juicebox protocol itself supports introducing independent third-party backends to distribute trust, but Garrett's analysis found no substantial deployment of this feature in X. Even more critically, XChat's protocol lacks "forward secrecy." This means that if your static key is compromised one day—whether your device is stolen, your key is seized, or the server reassembles and decrypts it—all your past chat history will be instantly accessible. Signal's "Double Ratchet" algorithm ensures that even if a communication key is leaked once, your history remains secure. XChat does not have this mechanism. Furthermore, by checking the privacy labels disclosed by Apple's App Store, users discovered that XChat retains the right to collect and associate data with user identities, covering contact information, communication content, usage data, diagnostic data, and user IDs. In contrast, Signal only collects a very small amount of contact information necessary for registration and never associates it with individual identities. A deeper issue lies in metadata. XChat may encrypt the text and images you send, but the X platform records in its backend: who you chat with, the frequency of your chats, the most active time periods, and the size of the files transferred. In the contemporary data economy, the commercial value of metadata often exceeds that of the content itself. These behavioral trajectories can feed back into X's main site's advertising engine and are also excellent corpus for training Grok AI. In short, encrypted chat content and exposed behavioral data constitute XChat's biggest privacy paradox. The real intention is not what it seems; Musk's ambition for a super app is more than just an ad. To understand XChat's ambition, one must first understand what Musk truly wants to achieve. His elaborate efforts suggest he doesn't just want a simple chat tool, but a "super app" that integrates users' daily lives—a place to talk to friends, transfer money, and buy things. Following this logic, XChat is just the first step. It aims for deep integration with the upcoming X Money payment system, allowing users to complete cross-border remittances and daily transfers while sending messages, completely creating a closed-loop business model of "social + payment." However, the obstacle lies in regulation. The United States lacks a unified federal financial remittance license; applications must be submitted individually in each of the fifty states. As of early 2026, X Payments LLC had obtained licenses in over 40 states and Washington, D.C., but New York State, the heart of North American finance, remains closed to Musk.

New York State Senator Brad Hoylman-Sigal and Representative Micah Lasher jointly submitted an open letter to the New York Department of Financial Services, using strong language, demanding that a license be denied to X, arguing that Musk's "behavior is seriously lacking in character and general suitability." For a payment network aiming for the entire US and even the world, losing New York State means XChat's payment network can no longer cover the most affluent consumers in the country. Moreover, Western users are inherently averse to "putting all their eggs in one basket," and if payment functionality is further compromised, the story becomes even more untenable. While these setbacks certainly raise doubts about the "super app" narrative, once the underlying logic is understood, everything becomes reasonable.

Leaving aside the geeky hype about self-destructing messages and encrypted privacy, the most imaginative rumor about XChat right now is how it will integrate with its own AI big model, Grok.

While we can't actually verify this yet, if we follow this line of thought, you'll find that what Musk truly wants to disrupt isn't the chat experience, but the underlying logic of human-computer interaction—that is, what should a super app look like in the AI ​​era? WeChat's super app model can be summarized as "entry point aggregation": one app crams in travel, food delivery, payment, social security, and health, allowing users to navigate between different services within a single interface. This model has essentially defined the product paradigm of the Chinese internet over the past decade. But its underlying logic remains "you come to find services." You know you need a ride, so you click on the Didi mini-program; you know you need to pay, so you open WeChat Pay. However, entry point aggregation is the answer for super apps in the app era. AI scheduling may be the true answer to the super app of the AI ​​era. Instead of cramming a hundred functions into users, let one AI handle everything for them. Of course, based on current leaks, XChat is still far from this vision. Without a rich service ecosystem to support it, even the smartest Grok can only do menial tasks like translation and text summarization within the chat box. Musk's answer may be rough and controversial, but he has begun to submit his paper.

data.title: Assassination list exposed: 20-year-old suspect becomes killer "to save all mankind"
data.description:
There's a scene in the sci-fi anime *Pantheon* where the anti-AI organization "AIDAS" obtains a list of all the scientists and executives promoting consciousness-uploading technology, and then takes action against them one by one.

When watching, viewers felt this was an extreme setting in the animation.

4 AM, April 11, 2026, San Francisco.

San Francisco.

A 20-year-old Texas boy named Daniel Moreno-Gama, wearing a Champion hoodie and carrying a kerosene canister, stood in front of Ultraman's multi-million dollar mansion, lit a Molotov cocktail, and threw it out.

An hour and a half later, he appeared at OpenAI headquarters, picked up a chair, smashed the glass door, and shouted at the security guards, "I'm going to burn this place down and kill everyone inside."

At that moment, the Pantheon pierced into reality.

The animated film became a real news headline. The assassination list went from fiction to reality.

The animated film became a real news headline. The assassination list went from fiction to reality.

Suspect Profile: From PauseAI Forum to Molotov Cocktails

Suspect Profile: From PauseAI Forum to Molotov Cocktails

This boy is named Daniel Moreno-Gama, 20 years old, from Spring, Texas.

Suspect Daniel Moreno-Gama, wearing a black Champion hoodie and holding a transparent container resembling a kerosene can, stands at the building entrance. This blurry screenshot almost completely overlaps with the silhouette of the AIDAS killer in Pantheon, sending chills down one's spine.

He flew from Texas to San Francisco specifically for this one night.

The FBI found the complete tools used in the crime on him: a fire extinguisher, a can of kerosene, a blue lighter, and a chilling "anti-AI manifesto." The manifesto's title was clearly "Your Final Warning," followed by his signature. It explicitly stated the intention to "kill/attempt to kill" Victim 1, namely OpenAI CEO Altman. Even more terrifying is this statement: "If I were to encourage others to kill and commit crimes, then I must lead by example and demonstrate that my message is entirely sincere." This is not the ramblings of a madman. This is a declaration of war. On Monday, FBI agents went to Daniel Moreno-Gama's home in Spring, a suburb of Houston, Texas, and stayed there for several hours before leaving. The investigation revealed that two years ago, he joined PauseAI's Discord forum and posted 34 messages. While he didn't directly make any threats, one of them was flagged as a "vague threat." PauseAI's goal is to persuade governments to intervene and suspend the development of Superhuman AI. The non-profit organization that called for a halt to cutting-edge AI development is now a prime target for FBI investigation. From keyboard rage to real-life flames, he finally took that step. That list: Silicon Valley's "Schindler's List" turned upside down. What truly kept Silicon Valley up at night was another part of the list. It was a detailed list. It listed the names and home addresses of numerous AI company board members, CEOs, and investors. This is no longer simply a matter of personal venting. It's a cold-blooded "target selection logic"—first target the most representative individuals, then incite more people to follow suit. Federal prosecutors have issued a strong statement: if it is proven that his intention was to coerce the government or change public policy, this case will be directly classified as domestic terrorism. The AIDAS organization from *The Pantheon* is now a reality in Silicon Valley. The distance between fiction and reality has been reduced to zero. This is not an isolated incident. This is a clear turning point where AI panic has escalated from online petitions and street protests to offline violence. Movements like PauseAI originally called for a halt and for safety. Today, some people interpret "safety" as something that must be achieved using kerosene and flames. Public warnings from Anthropic executives and others—that AI could lead to 30%-50% of human unemployment—are objectively providing extremists with a "moral license." A dangerous logical loop has formed: AI threatens human survival → stopping AI is justice → stopping AI by force is also necessary justice. This landslide has already reached the bottom for some people. When the speed of technological advancement exceeds the psychological capacity of society, fear begins to backfire.

AI overhype has backfired

Do you think AI is just a tool?

Now, it has become a "human extinction machine" in the eyes of some people.

Their chosen method of response was true "extermination"—first eliminating those who instigated it. Who is the next target? Ultraman has confirmed the attack. He even posted a photo of his husband and young child, writing, "Hopefully this will deter the next person who wants to throw a Molotov cocktail at my house...whatever you think of me." He acknowledged that "fear and anxiety about AI are reasonable," but emphasized the need to "reduce rhetoric and tactics to prevent further explosions in more homes." Security levels in Silicon Valley are being urgently upgraded. But those names on the list are probably having a hard time sleeping right now. The deeper question is: When those who fear AI begin to respond with violence, and those who are pushing AI forward continue to accelerate recklessly—who will apply the brakes? This fear and confrontation triggered by technology has only just entered its most dangerous phase. And who will be the next target?

data.title: A Triumph of Values: How Anthropic Overtakes OpenAI
data.description: This might be the most thrilling AI revenge drama of the year. OpenAI, once the undisputed king of big data analytics, is no longer the dominant force it once was. Its former employees, along with six others, have left to found Anthropic, which is eroding OpenAI's leading position in terms of revenue, valuation, and market share. The contrast in the secondary market is most striking. Ken Smythe, founder of Next Round Capital, has $600 million in applications to sell existing OpenAI shares, with six hedge funds and venture capital firms lining up to sell. At this time last year, these shares would have been snapped up within days. Now? He's sifted through a pool of hundreds of institutional investors, but can't find a single buyer. At the same time, $2 billion in cash is queuing up to buy Anthropic shares. On the on-chain derivatives platform Ventuals, Anthropic's implied valuation briefly surpassed OpenAI's, at $863.6 billion to $846.1 billion. Goldman Sachs' attitude is even more telling. Selling existing OpenAI shares to high-net-worth clients without taking a share of the profits is essentially a discounted fire sale. But selling Anthropic shares? They still take a 15% to 20% carry, take it or leave it. How did Anthropic, founded only five years ago, gradually surpass its former parent company, OpenAI? The story begins in 2020. That year, Dario Amodei was the VP of Research at OpenAI, involved in building GPT-2 and GPT-3. Many versions circulated in Silicon Valley regarding his departure; some said it was because Microsoft's investment changed the nature of OpenAI, others said it was due to fundamental differences in security philosophy. Dario himself discussed this issue on Lex Fridman's podcast, essentially saying: arguing about other people's visions is extremely inefficient; instead of trying to change others, take the people you trust and do what you want to do. In 2021, Dario left with his sister Daniela and five other core OpenAI researchers to found Anthropic. Sam Altman probably didn't pay much attention at the time. OpenAI was at its peak then; losing a few researchers wasn't a big deal. However, at the height of the "board coup" in November 2023, OpenAI's board even approached Dario, asking if he would be willing to replace Altman as CEO and, incidentally, merge the two companies. Dario refused. He didn't want the OpenAI CEO position; he wanted to build something from scratch according to his own logic. From 2021 to 2024, Anthropic was virtually invisible to the outside world. When ChatGPT exploded globally at the end of 2022, Claude was still in internal testing. The Anthropic team believed that safety was more important than functionality. This insistence caused them to miss the first wave of AI hype, but it also gave them a unique position in the subsequent competition. A Comeback! The numbers speak for themselves. Anthropic's Annualized Revenue (ARR): $1 billion in January 2025, $9 billion by the end of the year, $14 billion in February 2026, $19 billion in March, and surpassing $30 billion in early April. OpenAI's comparison: approximately $13 billion in 2025, and approximately $25 billion by April 2026. Anthropic grew 30 times in 15 months, going from lagging behind OpenAI by an order of magnitude to surpassing it by 20%. OpenAI's own growth is also quite rapid, but compared to Anthropic, it becomes a contrast of "steady growth vs. exponential growth." The biggest structural difference lies in the fact that over 80% of OpenAI's revenue comes from ChatGPT consumer subscriptions. 900 million weekly active users—the numbers are impressive—but the paid subscription rate is only around 5%, with the remaining 95% simply using computing power for free. Anthropic, on the other hand, derives 80% of its revenue from enterprise clients and API calls. Enterprise revenue and consumer revenue are completely different things. Enterprise contracts are difficult to switch to once signed; deep integration incurs switching costs, resulting in high renewal rates and year-on-year increases in subscription amounts. Consumer subscriptions can be canceled at any time; a new feature can cause a significant loss of subscribers. In trading terms, one is a long-duration asset, and the other is a short-duration asset. Let's look at some specific data. By April 2026, Anthropic had over 1,000 enterprise clients with annual fees exceeding $1 million, doubling in two months. Eight of the Fortune 10 companies use Claude. In the core field of code generation, Claude captured 42% to 54% of the global market share, while OpenAI only had 21%. Ramp's enterprise spending data shows that Anthropic's share of enterprise AI spending surged from 10% at the beginning of 2025 to over 65% in February 2026. Do these figures mean OpenAI is "failing"? Not necessarily. But they do illustrate one thing: the first-mover advantage that everyone thought was unshakeable a year ago—brand, user base, and ecosystem—has played almost no role in the enterprise market; enterprise purchasing decisions follow a different logic. The catalyst for Anthropic's explosive revenue growth was a product called Claude Code. Released in May 2025, it surpassed $1 billion in annualized revenue by November, and exceeded $2.5 billion by February 2026. A product went from zero to $2.5 billion in just nine months. Looking back at the history of the SaaS industry, there's no faster example. Cursor took over a year to reach $500 million, and GitHub Copilot took even longer. What exactly is the difference between Claude Code and previous AI programming tools? Simply put, GitHub Copilot completes the next line of code for you while you write it, so you're still the one doing the work. Claude Code, on the other hand, lets you tell it "I want a user login module," and it writes the code, creates the files, runs tests, and commits changes all by itself, while you watch from the sidelines. This difference may seem like a matter of degree, but it's actually a paradigm shift: one is a "better tool," the other is a "colleague who replaces you in doing the work." Anthropic's internal data further illustrates this point. Boris Cherny, the head of Claude Code, says that 100% of his daily code is now written using Claude Code, and 70% to 90% of the entire engineering team's code is generated by it. 90% of Claude Code's own codebase is also written by themselves. A Pragmatic Engineer survey of 15,000 developers in February 2026 ranked Claude Code as the most popular AI coding tool. By early 2026, 4% of public commits on GitHub were made using Claude Code, and this figure was projected to exceed 20% by the end of the year. Claude Code's success reveals a reality many in the AI ​​industry are unwilling to face: the commercial ceiling for chatbots themselves may be quite low. What truly motivates companies to invest heavily are AI tools that are embedded in workflows and replace specific job functions. ChatGPT opened the door to AI, but whether one chooses to go left or right after entering determines who can turn users into revenue. Anthropic went right, entering the production process of enterprises. In January 2026, Anthropic released Cowork, extending the same approach from developers to all white-collar jobs. Four engineers built it in 10 days, with most of the code written by Claude Code himself. Since the launch of Claude Cowork, the global SaaS sector has lost approximately $2 trillion in market value. The differences between product and strategy are obvious, but the real key lies in: people. Looking at OpenAI, from 2024 to 2025, the company experienced a systemic exodus of senior executives. Co-founder and Chief Scientist Ilya Sutskever left to found Safe Superintelligence. CTO Mira Murati left to found Thinking Machines Lab. Co-founder John Schulman and Super Alignment Team leader Jan Leike went to Anthropic. Chief Research Officer Bob McGrew left, VP of Research Barret Zoph left, and co-founder and president Greg Brockman took an extended leave of absence. In the summer of 2025, at least seven researchers were poached by Meta's Super Intelligence Lab. Of OpenAI's original 11 co-founders, only Sam Altman and researcher Wojciech Zaremba remained full-time by the end of 2025. A former employee told Fortune: "OpenAI without Ilya is a different company; OpenAI without Greg is a very different company." Anthropic presents a different picture. All seven co-founders—Dario Amodei, Daniela Amodei, Jared Kaplan, Jack Clark, Sam McCandlish, Ben Mann, and Tom Brown—are still active, and there hasn't been a single publicly announced departure at the executive level in the five years since the company's founding. This contrast is stark, prompting the question: What exactly did Anthropic do to keep them on the team? Forbes estimated in early 2026 that each of the seven co-founders would hold approximately 1.8% of the shares, a very small difference. At a valuation of $380 billion, each person's shares would be worth approximately $6.8 billion. This near-equal shareholding structure is completely different from the common practice in Silicon Valley, where the CEO typically holds the majority, with the remaining founders receiving progressively less. Equal shareholding eliminates at least one of the most common sources of conflict within the founding team: who feels they are being shortchanged. Equity is just the surface; what's more noteworthy is Dario Amodei's time commitment to management. He mentioned on the Dwarkesh Podcast that he spends about one-third to 40% of his time "ensuring the culture at Anthropic is good." This is unusually high for an AI company CEO. As the company expanded to 2,500 people, he could no longer participate in every technical and product decision, so he chose to focus his energy on something more "leveraged": aligning everyone's direction. How exactly does he do this? He holds a company-wide meeting every two weeks, internally called "DVQ"—Dario Vision Quest. This name was given by employees; Dario himself once considered changing it because it sounded like a hallucinogenic experience. For each meeting, he prepares three or four pages of documentation and speaks for an hour in front of the entire company, covering topics ranging from product strategy and geopolitics to his assessment of the AI ​​industry's future. Most employees attend in person or remotely. On a more routine level, Anthropic has a Slack culture akin to "notebook channels." Every employee, including Dario himself, maintains a public Slack channel to share their thoughts, work progress, and even questions. In an interview with Fortune, he said something to the effect of: "My goal is to build a reputation for 'telling the company the truth,' directly pointing out problems and avoiding 'corporate speak' (that defensive, politically correct corporate language). If you hire people you trust, you can communicate completely unfiltered." This "anti-PR" style of internal communication contrasts sharply with OpenAI. During OpenAI's board crisis at the end of 2023, internal information was so fragmented that even the CTO was unsure what was happening. Anthropic's cultural filtering begins from the recruitment stage. Every candidate, regardless of the position they apply for, undergoes a standardized "culture interview." Only employees who have been with the company for at least 30 days and completed multi-stage culture training are qualified to serve as culture interviewers. The logic is: cultural transmission is too important to be entrusted to someone who doesn't even understand what the company culture is. Reportedly, one question in the culture interview was: If Anthropic decides not to release a model due to its inability to guarantee security, and your equity is reduced to zero, would you be willing to accept it? This isn't a rhetorical question; no matter how strong your technical skills, if you can't answer this question, you won't be hired. Another detail: All technical positions at Anthropic, from new hires to founding executives, use the same title: "Member of Technical Staff." There are no hierarchical distinctions like "Senior," "Chief," or "Distinguished." Employees internally refer to each other as "ants" (an abbreviation of Anthropic). The company even hired a full-time philosopher, Amanda Askell, whose job was to shape Claude's framework of moral judgment. She once told Time: "Sometimes it feels like you have a 6-year-old child, and you're teaching it what kindness is, but when it's 15, it's smarter than you in every way." Daniela Amodei's role in this system is often underestimated. Dario is the technical visionary and external advocate, while Daniela oversees execution, culture, talent, and operational infrastructure. According to reports, her executive teams in research, product, sales, and operations all report directly to her. She has a clear preference in hiring: people with strong communication skills, high emotional intelligence, kindness, curiosity, and a willingness to help others. This emphasis on "soft skills" is uncommon in an industry dominated by tech founders. All seven founders of Anthropic have signed a pledge to donate 80% of their wealth, and nearly 30 Anthropic employees have registered to attend the 2026 EA (Effective Altruism) conference in San Francisco—more than double the combined attendance of OpenAI, Google DeepMind, xAI, and Meta Superintelligence Labs. The core asset of an AI company is the human brain. Code can be copied, computing power can be bought, but the intuition and judgment of researchers cannot be taken away. When your chief scientist, CTO, and chief research officer leave within two years, what you lose cannot be measured in terms of funding. Anthropic's stability in terms of talent is perhaps its most difficult advantage to replicate. All victories are victories of values. What happened to OpenAI? At this point, I need to say a few words in defense of OpenAI. Anthropic's revenue has surpassed OpenAI's, and secondary market sentiment is shifting. But OpenAI hasn't collapsed. It just completed a $122 billion funding round, with participants including Amazon, Nvidia, SoftBank, and Microsoft. ChatGPT still has 900 million weekly active users. In consumers' minds, "AI" and "ChatGPT" are almost synonymous, but OpenAI does have some structural problems, and these problems will all surface in 2026. The financial pressure is the most direct. OpenAI projects a loss of $14 billion in 2026. Cumulative losses from 2023 to 2028 could reach as high as $44 billion. HSBC analysts believe profitability will not arrive before 2030. The Wall Street Journal estimates that OpenAI's annual training costs will reach $125 billion by 2030, compared to approximately $30 billion for Anthropic during the same period. Both are training cutting-edge models, yet the costs differ by four times; this gap needs explanation. Part of the reason is OpenAI's more aggressive investment in computing infrastructure, and part of it may be efficiency issues. The capital market is clearly very concerned about this gap. Anthropic expects to achieve positive cash flow in 2027, while OpenAI has pushed its break-even point to 2030. There have also been some issues at the product level. Sora shut down in March 2026. This video generation tool reportedly had operating costs of $15 million per day and total revenue of $2.1 million. The shutdown also ruined a partnership with Disney, reportedly canceling a $1 billion investment deal. OpenAI's new AGI deployment head, Fidji Simo, bluntly told employees that the company "cannot afford to be distracted by side projects." Then there's advertising. In February 2026, OpenAI added ads to the free and Go versions of ChatGPT. This event itself isn't big news; many products have advertising models. However, it's particularly jarring in the context of OpenAI because Sam Altman explicitly stated in 2024 that advertising was a "last resort," and that the combination of advertising and AI made him "uniquely uneasy." From this "unique unease" to the "official launch," only 15 months passed. With only 5% of 900 million users paying, this figure forced him to make this choice. The corporate governance aspect is even more complex. The restructuring from non-profit to for-profit dragged on for nearly a year. There was Elon Musk's lawsuit, a joint letter of opposition from former employees, an open letter signed by a Nobel laureate, and reviews from the attorneys general of California and Delaware. The restructuring was finally completed in October 2025, with the non-profit foundation retaining a 26% stake and control. Critics argue that this arrangement is merely a formality. Individually, none of these events are fatal. Taken together, they paint a less-than-ideal picture: a company that once led the industry's imagination is now filled with internal governance struggles, product shutdowns, and advertising. Anthropic's momentum is indeed formidable. Revenue surpassing others, secondary market frenzy, and free global PR from the Pentagon incident. But one thing is worth remembering: if you asked any industry analyst at the end of 2023, "Will OpenAI be surpassed?", 99% would say no. The rapid shift in consensus should itself raise concerns about the current new consensus. A few things are relatively certain. Anthropic's enterprise-centric approach is correct; its 80% enterprise revenue structure is far healthier than ChatGPT's consumer model, supported by robust financial data. Claude Code is a genuine product breakthrough, achieving $2.5 billion in ARR in just 9 months—this speed speaks volumes. But there are just as many uncertainties. OpenAI boasts 900 million weekly active users and the strongest AI brand recognition globally. If it finds an effective way to monetize consumers, even just increasing the paid subscription rate from 5% to 10%, the entire story will have to be rewritten. The AI ​​industry has a characteristic that makes prediction dangerous: a major model breakthrough can reshape the landscape. Secondary market fund flows are indeed telling us a direction, but the secondary market has also chased WeWork. A more restrained conclusion is that in the first round of AI commercialization, Anthropic's path has been proven, while OpenAI's path is being questioned. But the phrase "the outcome is decided" is still premature; this battle is only halfway through. When Dario Amodei left OpenAI with six people in 2021, probably no one could have predicted this situation. A person with a background in security research, in an industry where everyone is racing against time, has managed to force his former employer into a situation where they need to write memos to investors explaining their competitive advantage, all while using less money and more self-discipline. The most interesting thing about this story is that it's still going strong.

data.title: US military blockade of Hormuz: Six key market concerns
data.description: The US blockade of the Strait of Hormuz pushes the global energy landscape to a new critical point. According to Xinhua News Agency, Trump announced on April 12 that the US Navy would immediately blockade all ships entering and leaving the Strait of Hormuz, and the US military officially implemented the blockade on April 13. This move occurred just hours after the breakdown of US-Iran negotiations in Islamabad, directly jeopardizing the fragile ceasefire agreement reached on April 7. The Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps immediately warned that any military vessel approaching the Strait of Hormuz under any pretext would be considered a violation of the ceasefire agreement, and threatened to strike all ports in the Persian Gulf and the Sea of ​​Oman if its ports were threatened. Oil prices surged in response, with the market pricing in further supply shortages. If the blockade is effectively enforced, it will not only cut off Iran's lifeline for oil exports but also exacerbate the pressure on Asian countries heavily reliant on Middle Eastern energy. Below are the six core issues that investors are most concerned about. What exactly is the US Navy doing? On April 12, the permanent ceasefire negotiations between the US and Iran in Islamabad broke down. According to CCTV International News, hours later, Trump posted on social media that, "effective immediately," the US Navy would blockade "any ship attempting to enter or leave the Strait of Hormuz," and indicated that other countries would participate, but without naming them. He also threatened to "intercept all ships paying passage fees to Iran in international waters," implying that the blockade might extend far beyond the strait itself, reaching as far as the Sea of ​​Oman. Compared to Trump's rhetoric, the US military's official statement was slightly narrower. The military set the start time for the blockade at 10:00 AM New York time on April 13th, applying to all "ships entering and leaving Iranian ports and coastal areas." Neutral vessels that had not docked in Iran were not obstructed, but might be subject to inspection for prohibited cargo. The US military advised crew members navigating in the Sea of ​​Oman and approaching the Strait of Hormuz to closely monitor official announcements and proactively contact the US Navy. On April 13th, the US military notified shipping operators that it would intercept, expel, or seize vessels departing from Iran. What remains unclear is whether the United States is willing to send its warships deep into the Indian Ocean to pursue oil tankers, and how the two sides will respond in the event of a potential standoff or damage to the tankers. How will Iran respond? According to CCTV News, after the United States threatened to begin preventing any ships from entering or leaving the Strait of Hormuz, the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps stated that any military vessel attempting to approach the strait under any pretext would be considered a violation of the ceasefire agreement. Iran also warned that if its shipping hubs are threatened, it will launch attacks on all ports in the Persian Gulf and the Sea of ​​Oman. John Bradford, a former U.S. Navy officer and co-founder of the Yokosuka Asia-Pacific Research Council, stated: "This new escalation is more likely to trigger further confrontation than to promote reconciliation." Why did the U.S. take this action? Since the U.S.-Israel strikes against Iran on February 28, Tehran has continuously tightened its control over the Strait of Hormuz, reducing the number of daily transit vessels from approximately 135 during peacetime to single digits, nearly paralyzing this vital waterway. This asymmetric tactic has proven quite challenging for the United States—Iran successfully blocked the passage of foreign ships while maintaining its own oil exports, thus both driving up global oil prices and preserving export revenues. The ultimate goal of the blockade is to cut off Iran's oil exports and sever its regime's key source of revenue. The Trump administration had previously implemented this strategy in Venezuela, using a blockade combined with sanctions to cripple the country's economy. However, Venezuela is far smaller than Iran, has a limited fleet, and its strategic significance to global oil exports is far less than Iran's. What does this mean for Iran? If the blockade is effectively enforced, it will be a heavy blow to Iran, which is highly dependent on oil exports. In recent weeks, Iran has benefited from high oil prices—latest data shows that Iran's oil revenue surged 37% in March. Crude oil previously sold at a discount was traded at a premium earlier this month, partly due to waivers granted by the US to increase supply, allowing buyers to purchase previously sanctioned Iranian crude. For Iran, which is facing severe damage from US-Israeli airstrikes and urgently needs to rebuild its economy, higher prices per barrel are crucial. Since the outbreak of the war, this extra profit has accumulated to hundreds of millions of dollars. However, once the blockade is implemented, this windfall may come to an abrupt end. What does this mean for the United States? Trump has frequently linked the Middle East supply shocks to the prospects of US oil and gas exports, characterizing this crisis as a strategic opportunity for the US, a top oil producer. According to CCTV International News, Trump claimed on Monday that a large number of oil tankers are already heading to the US, loading oil and shipping it globally, "these ships don't even need to pass through the Strait of Hormuz." However, US crude oil is not a perfect substitute for Middle Eastern oils, and for US consumers, high benchmark oil prices are already pushing up inflation. Iran clearly understands that it may be more resilient than the United States in withstanding economic pressure. Oil prices surged immediately after news of the blockade broke, as investors prepared for potential supply shortages. What does this mean for Asian countries? Asia is the biggest victim of this energy crisis, and further restrictions on traffic in the Strait of Hormuz will exacerbate the region's situation. The number of ships passing through the Strait had already decreased significantly prior to the blockade announcement. The waivers previously granted by the United States for purchasing sanctioned Iranian crude oil appear to have been nullified by the blockade, drastically reducing any room for maneuver. Countries that previously reached bilateral agreements with Iran may now be hesitant to cut back on their purchasing options due to concerns about a direct confrontation with the United States. South Korean President Lee Jae-myung previously urged the nation to "save every drop of fuel" and proposed a $17 billion emergency aid plan to mitigate the impact of fuel price shocks on South Korean households and businesses. Meanwhile, economies heavily reliant on Gulf energy supplies, such as India and Australia, have also taken emergency measures. Jorge Montepeque, Managing Director of Onyx Capital Group, stated bluntly in an interview with Bloomberg Television: "They're so focused on Iran that they can't see what they're doing to the whole world. The pain is in Asia, the pain is in the South Pacific, the pain is in all oil-dependent countries."

data.title: The battle for stablecoin licenses has ended: anxious Hong Kong will not see another Ma Ying-jeou.
data.description:
Author: Sleepy.md

In July 2024, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) announced the list of three participants in its stablecoin sandbox.

One ​​of the three is called Yuancoin Technology, founded by Norman Chan. This name is well-known in Hong Kong's financial circles; he served as Chief Executive of the HKMA for a full ten years, personally creating Hong Kong's current financial regulatory framework. After retiring, he started his own business, entering the sandbox he designed with $40 million in funding.

Two years later, in April 2026, the first batch of stablecoin licenses was announced. Norman Chan was not selected. This April, the issuance of Hong Kong stablecoin licenses was packaged as a milestone in financial innovation.

But if you peel back the glamorous narratives of "embracing Web3," you'll see a completely different story. This license exposes Hong Kong's predicament at the turn of the era. This city, once perfectly shaped by its history, is now trapped by that same heavy history. The competition for Hong Kong's stablecoin licenses is more like an arranged marriage with a predetermined outcome from the start. A total of 36 institutions submitted applications for stablecoin licenses, forming a long queue, including tech giants, established securities firms, and Web3 native startups with substantial financial backing. However, only two licenses were ultimately issued, a success rate of just 5.5%. Among the companies that failed to make the cut were Yuanbi Technology, founded by former HKMA Chief Executive Norman Chan, JD CoinChain (a former sandbox participant), and OSL, Hong Kong's largest licensed virtual asset exchange. Those institutions that harbored strategic ambitions and brought hot money to try and expand their territory in the digital currency wave ultimately failed. So, who got the ticket? One was HSBC. This long-established institution, which has been issuing banknotes in Hong Kong for 160 years, plans to launch a Hong Kong dollar stablecoin in the second half of 2026 and integrate it into PayMe and its mobile banking app. Its approach to the Web3 world is to carefully put new things into its most familiar old bottles. The other company is Dingdian Fintech. This is a hastily assembled company created to obtain a license, with Standard Chartered Bank holding 50.5%, Animoca Brands 37.5%, and HKT 12%. Standard Chartered needed compliance backing, HKT valued payment scenarios, and Animoca craved on-chain channels. None of the three parties were confident they could handle this difficult task alone, so they chose to band together. Both of these approved institutions are, without exception, old guard within the traditional financial system and are both note-issuing banks. Why did Hong Kong regulators issue this license, representing the future of financial infrastructure, to those who seemed to need it the least? Why did those passionate entrepreneurs fail? The answer may be quite realistic. In the eyes of regulators, stablecoins have never been a business, but rather an infrastructure. And infrastructure is destined to be entrusted only to those "their own people" who know it best. Limited returns, unlimited risks. The regulatory threshold for stablecoin licenses in Hong Kong is so high that only note-issuing banks can meet the requirements. But once you're actually at the table, you'll find that it's actually a business with almost no profit. According to Hong Kong's Stablecoin Ordinance, issuers are required to maintain 100% high-quality asset reserves. This means that for every HK$100 of stablecoins issued, HK$100 in cash or short-term government bonds must be safely stored in banks. This money cannot be used for lending or pursuing high returns. At the same time, the issuer must maintain a minimum paid-up capital of HK$25 million, operate cautiously under stringent bank-level anti-money laundering standards, and promise to respond to users' redemption requests within one business day. We can compare this to virtual banks in Hong Kong. Hong Kong currently has eight fully licensed virtual banks that can offer high-interest loans and invest in securities. However, since their opening in 2020, none of these eight banks have been profitable, with total losses reaching billions of Hong Kong dollars in 2024. None of them have achieved profitability since their inception. Even fully licensed virtual banks are struggling with losses, so the plight of stablecoin issuers, who can only buy short-term government bonds and live off meager interest, is imaginable. They bear the unlimited responsibility of maintaining currency stability and silently swallow the high costs of compliance and technological infrastructure. Ultimately, this is a business with limited returns and unlimited risks. In this game, Standard Chartered and HSBC can hardly be considered true winners; they can be understood as being forced into the game. If HSBC didn't apply, it would be tantamount to handing over the underlying infrastructure of the digital Hong Kong dollar to Standard Chartered; if Standard Chartered didn't apply, it would be tantamount to admitting its absence from the future landscape of Hong Kong's financial system. Through high compliance thresholds and unspoken rules, the Hong Kong government has firmly locked these two note-issuing banks at the table. Through this ingenious rule design, regulators have made the giants "voluntarily" bear the enormous cost of building digital currency infrastructure. Those familiar with the city know that this is actually the consistent modus operandi of Hong Kong regulators. But where does this extreme aversion to risk and preference for confining innovation within a cage originate? Post-traumatic stress disorder. Hong Kong's extreme conservatism regarding stablecoins has been criticized as stifling innovation. However, if you examine Hong Kong's financial history, you'll find that this conservatism isn't due to the current regulators' timidity, but rather a muscle memory shaped by the city's bloody lessons learned from several life-or-death crises. Behind every stringent regulatory clause lies a real crisis. The first crisis was in 1983. That year, Sino-British negotiations were deadlocked. Extreme political uncertainty directly triggered a crisis of confidence in the Hong Kong dollar. Citizens frantically sold Hong Kong dollars and bought US dollars, causing the Hong Kong dollar exchange rate to plummet from around 5 Hong Kong dollars to 9.6 Hong Kong dollars in just a few days. Toilet paper and canned goods in supermarkets were sold out, and panic spread throughout Hong Kong. On that turbulent weekend, the Hong Kong British government urgently implemented the linked exchange rate system, announcing that the Hong Kong dollar would be firmly "pegged" to 7.8 Hong Kong dollars to 1 US dollar. For every 7.8 Hong Kong dollars issued, the issuing bank had to deposit 1 US dollar into the Exchange Fund. They attempted to use absolute US dollar reserves to exchange for absolute public confidence. This hastily decided decision during the crisis has been in effect for 43 years without wavering. It is the unshakeable anchor of Hong Kong's monetary and financial system, but it has also become the source of the city's conservatism. The second crisis was in 1997. The Asian financial crisis swept in, and international speculators led by George Soros, with huge sums of money, launched a third attack on the Hong Kong dollar. They used the linkage between the foreign exchange market, the stock market, and the futures market to try to break the linked exchange rate system. In that battle, which was fierce but without gunpowder, the Hong Kong government used HK$118 billion of the Exchange Fund to intervene in the market, at one point buying up 7% of the market value of Hong Kong stocks, finally managing to repel the short sellers. This was a brutal victory won with real money. The price it left for the city was an extreme sensitivity among regulators to systemic risks and liquidity shortages that became ingrained in their very being. The third crisis was in 2008. That year, the collapse of Lehman Brothers triggered a global financial tsunami. In Hong Kong, over 44,000 citizens lost everything after purchasing Lehman-related products, with the amount involved reaching HK$20.1 billion. Countless elderly people with gray hair gathered outside banks, weeping and protesting for months. This event left a deep, unhealed scar on Hong Kong society. This not only directly led to Hong Kong's stringent regulatory system for retail financial products, but also planted a deep-seated wariness and distrust of complex financial derivatives in the hearts of an entire generation of Hong Kong people. Looking into these three historical wounds, you might understand why the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) unhesitatingly imposed the world's most stringent requirement of "100% high-quality asset reserves" when dealing with stablecoins. From the regulators' perspective, no matter how cutting-edge the technology, stablecoins are ultimately privately issued currency stripped of national credit backing. If even a 1% gap appears in the stablecoin's reserve assets, and a run occurs, who will bail it out? Will ordinary taxpayers suffer the consequences, or will the government have to once again open up foreign exchange reserves to fill the hole? Faced with stablecoins, Hong Kong's first instinct has never been about embracing innovation, but rather "never letting a repeat of the collapse." This obsession with absolute security is more like a long-term collective post-traumatic stress response, ultimately manifesting as restrained ink, etched word by word into legal provisions. However, when a city pursues "security" to the extreme, what price does it pay? Hong Kong's predicament does not stem from backwardness, but rather from its past overreach. This city became accustomed to achieving excellence in a particular era, then was quietly trapped by that ultimate success, ultimately missing out on the next era. The most typical example is the Octopus card. In 1997, the Octopus card was launched in Hong Kong. It was one of the world's earliest and most successful contactless electronic payment systems, and was once eagerly adopted by major cities around the world. With this small card, you could use it almost effortlessly to ride the subway, take buses, buy newspapers, and eat fast food in Hong Kong. However, precisely because the Octopus card was so successful, so widespread, and so easy to use, Hong Kong merchants and consumers had no incentive to switch to new payment methods. While Alipay and WeChat Pay swept across mainland China with overwhelming force, reshaping the entire commercial landscape with QR codes, people in Hong Kong's subway stations and convenience stores still habitually swipe their Octopus cards. The success of Octopus cards delayed Hong Kong's entry into the mobile payment wave by a full decade. Now, faced with the rise of stablecoins and Web3, Hong Kong is replaying the Octopus card's story. Only this time, what's holding it back is its proud traditional financial system. Hong Kong boasts the world's most comprehensive traditional financial legal system, the most mature bank-issuing system, and the most stable and battle-tested linked exchange rate. These were Hong Kong's unparalleled moats in the era of traditional finance. But in the world of Web3, they have become its heaviest burden. Hong Kong attempted to embrace something new that aimed to disrupt the traditional financial system without altering its underlying structure. Its answer was to forcibly cram Web3 into the framework of traditional banking and then declare it "innovation." This is not only arrogance towards innovation but also an extreme fear of losing control. The city is so afraid of making mistakes that it would rather maintain an impeccable facade, watching an era rush by, than face the future with even the slightest flaw. Two Tracks, One City Hong Kong is conducting a "dual-track" financial experiment. Let's shift our focus from stablecoin licenses to the 400 Circle K convenience stores across Hong Kong. In October 2025, these convenience stores quietly added a new option at their checkout counters: support for digital yuan (e-CNY) payments. Accompanying this is the Hong Kong Monetary Authority's "Faster Payment System" and the digital yuan system completing the world's first two-way interoperability of legal digital currencies. And the main force driving all of this behind the scenes is Bank of China (Hong Kong). Now, look back at the list of stablecoin license applicants. Hong Kong has three note-issuing banks: HSBC, Standard Chartered, and Bank of China (Hong Kong). The first two have obtained licenses, but Bank of China (Hong Kong) is absent. The absence of Bank of China (Hong Kong) reveals that Hong Kong's financial foundation is being split into two parallel tracks. And these two tracks are each extending towards two completely different futures. One track leads to a Western perspective. Hong Kong is attempting to send a signal to international capital with a highly compliant Hong Kong dollar stablecoin license: it remains a transparent and strictly regulated international financial center, and in the dollar-dominated cryptocurrency landscape, Hong Kong still has the ability to carve out its own piece of the pie. Another track connects with the pulse of the mainland. The establishment of the digital yuan on the streets of Hong Kong carries the national strategy of RMB internationalization and reshaping cross-border payment and settlement. In this grand historical picture, Hong Kong must firmly shoulder this heavy responsibility and continue to play its irreplaceable role as a "super-connector." Those Chinese-funded institutions that quietly withdrew before the deadline for stablecoin license applications had long understood the distant future of these two tracks. In October 2025, according to the Financial Times, Ant Group and JD.com suspended their stablecoin plans in Hong Kong after receiving instructions from the People's Bank of China and the Cyberspace Administration of China to "temporarily suspend the project." The policy was finalized in February of the following year when the People's Bank of China, along with eight other departments, issued a notice, explicitly stating for the first time in a regulatory document that no entity or individual may issue stablecoins pegged to the RMB overseas; and that domestic entities and their controlled overseas entities may not issue virtual currencies overseas without approval. Faced with this clear red line, the liquidity of Hong Kong dollar stablecoins was inherently locked. It cannot flow north. The mainland has clearly defined virtual currencies as illegal financial activities, making it impossible for Hong Kong dollar stablecoins to become a channel for mainland funds to flow overseas. It also finds it difficult to move west. Looking across the ocean, USDT and USDC already dominate more than 85% of the global stablecoin market. With the gradual advancement of the US GENIUS Act, the moat surrounding US dollar stablecoins has already been built high. The fledgling Hong Kong dollar stablecoin has virtually no leverage to directly compete with the US dollar in international waters. Caught in the financial game between the world's two largest economies, Hong Kong attempts to maneuver within this narrow space using an extremely conservative and compliant approach. It must maintain the facade of the Western financial order while simultaneously shouldering the heavy responsibility entrusted by national strategy. This is no longer just a predicament over a stablecoin license; it's the anxieties Hong Kong, as a "super-connector," faces in the harsh winter of deglobalization. How long can a city run on two opposing tracks simultaneously? Hong Kong is not unaware of its situation. Every act of conservatism, every defensive move, is underpinned by a deep-seated obsession: I cannot afford to lose any more. At least on the surface, the lights of Victoria Harbour remain dazzling. In the latest Global Financial Centres Index released in March 2026, Hong Kong firmly holds its third-place position globally, with its banking and financing sectors topping the list. Undoubtedly, it remains a shining example of a top-tier global financial center. However, another set of data tells a completely different story. In 2025, the vacancy rate for Grade A office space in Hong Kong climbed to a record high of 17.5%. The total vacant office space in Hong Kong is equivalent to 13 buildings of the International Finance Centre Phase 2. Foreign financial institutions are continuing to lay off staff and scale back operations; APG, the Netherlands' largest pension fund, and several European and American law firms have successively reduced their operations in Hong Kong. By the first quarter of 2026, the Hang Seng Tech Index had plummeted 15.7%, ranking last among major global stock indices. Foreign capital continued to withdraw from Hong Kong's tech sector, with southbound capital becoming the only supporting force. Even the record that Hong Kong is so proud of—HK$285.8 billion raised through IPOs on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange in 2025, regaining its position as number one globally—reveals upon closer examination that nearly half of this HK$285.8 billion came from A-share companies listing in Hong Kong. This is less a reflection of global capital enthusiastically flowing into Hong Kong and more a reflection of mainland companies desperately seeking a breathing space for overseas financing. Hong Kong desperately wants to prove itself. It desperately wants to tell the world that it remains an irreplaceable financial center. In 1986, John Woo directed "A Better Tomorrow." Chow Yun-fat, playing Mark Gor, said in the film: "I've waited three years, waiting for an opportunity. I want to prove myself, not to show off, but to tell people that what I've lost, I will take back." That was 1986, the Sino-British negotiations had just concluded, and the entire city was filled with anxiety about the future and an intense yearning for dignity. Mark Gor's words, like a sharp knife, precisely struck the deepest emotions of that generation of Hong Kong people. Forty years later, Hong Kong is still waiting, waiting for an opportunity, waiting for a stage where it can prove itself again. This time, however, faced with the opportunity presented by Web3 and digital currencies—opportunities that could reshape the global financial landscape—it chose to seize them in the most conservative, safest, and least risky way. It personally locked its sharpest innovations within the strongest iron cage. Where has that once wild, adventurous Hong Kong gone? It dared to dance on the edge of a cliff, willing to pay any price to reclaim what it had lost.

data.title: YZi Labs has invested tens of millions of dollars in Genius, but it still lacks followers.
data.description:
Author: Chloe, ChainCatcher

On April 13th, Genius, the Perp DEX trading terminal supported by YZi Labs, disclosed information about its first quarter airdrop event, distributing a total of 70 million GENIUS tokens, and simultaneously launching on Binance Alpha. However, this launch, which should have been a celebratory moment, triggered a crisis of trust within the community, stemming from the huge gap between official rules and community expectations.

CZ's endorsement, billions in trading volume, are profit-seekers fully committed?

Genius is an on-chain perpetual contract trading terminal with tens of millions of dollars invested by YZi Labs and CZ personally serving as an advisor, and it has been surrounded by a halo from the beginning.

Its platform aggregates over 10 public chains and more than 150 DEXs, focusing on CEX-level execution speed and on-chain privacy transactions. In January of this year, it achieved a weekly trading volume exceeding $2 billion, with a cumulative total trading volume exceeding $15 billion. According to crypto asset data platform RootData, Genius completed a $6 million seed round of financing in October 2024, led by CMCC Global, with participation from AVA Labs, Arca, Flow Traders, and other institutions. Furthermore, according to Genius co-founder Armaan Kalsi, YZi Labs joined the funding round this year, investing "tens of millions of dollars" in Genius Trading, and CZ also joined the company as an advisor. According to previous official announcements, 21% of the total supply of Genius tokens was allocated to community airdrops, to be carried out in three seasons, with 7% allocated in each season. Users could accumulate Genius Points (GP) through spot trading, with points directly corresponding to token allocations. The platform did indeed reward the market with real money during the distribution process, accumulating over $7 million in cash returns and $1.3 million in referral rewards, distributed weekly in USDC. This mechanism operated smoothly in the early stages, and the expectations for many arbitrageurs were quite clear. However, when the rules were suddenly rewritten on the eve of TGE, these investments instantly became sunk costs.

70% of the allocated user quota was burned, simply because the official team wanted to filter long-term holders

Season 1 officially ended on April 12th at 22:00 EST. The official announcement simultaneously revealed the airdrop details: a total of 70 million GENIUS tokens were allocated, but there were only two ways to claim them: 1) Claim immediately, which users could do within 7 days after TGE, but the system automatically burned 70% of the allocated quota, leaving users with only 30%; 2) Do not claim and wait one year, at which time the tokens would be locked in a smart contract, and 100% could be claimed after one year. The official narrative was "to filter long-term holders and reduce initial selling pressure."

The community, of course, was not buying it.

For active users who have already contributed a significant amount of transaction fees, these two options are essentially a dilemma: either accept the reality of a 30% discount on the tokens, or gamble a year's worth of time on a project with an uncertain future. Unsurprisingly, a large number of criticisms such as "anti-freeze," "scam," and "backstabbing" quickly emerged on X, with related tweets garnering tens of thousands of views within hours, and community discussions increasing nearly fourfold overnight. According to community users' estimates, Genius's opening FDV needs to reach at least $800 million for recent entrants to possibly break even. For a project that has been online for several months and still has some incomplete features, this threshold is clearly too high. "Genius is the last straw for freezer traders recently." "Refund?" Users are also unable to receive their full transaction fees. Faced with strong backlash from the community, the Genius team responded in less than 24 hours. In the early hours of April 13th, the official team issued announcements on X and Telegram, stating that they had "listened to community feedback" and added a third option to the airdrop file: a refund. Users are offered the option to burn 100% of their token allocation within 48 hours of the TGE, in exchange for their net transaction fees generated on the Genius platform. The refund will arrive within 48 hours of the application. However, there are still limitations to the "fee refund," and the final amount a user receives may differ from the fees they actually paid. Genius's refund calculation involves two filters. First, the platform has given back over $8.3 million to the community in the past few months through cash rebates and referral rewards. These previously received earnings will be deducted from the refund—meaning the more transactions and rebates received, the less will be refunded. For highly active heavy users, the net amount may be significantly reduced. Second, not all on-chain transaction fees go to Genius. The fees for each transaction are actually split among multiple stages, including the underlying DEX protocol (such as PancakeSwap). Genius, as an aggregation layer, only takes a small portion, and the refund is strictly limited to the portion credited to Genius's own account. In response, the official stance is clear: they will directly refute any accusations of "not fully refunding" the fees, arguing that those fees never belonged to Genius in the first place. Furthermore, the team has maintained an appeal channel for users with doubts about the refund amount; within the first week after TGE, users can submit objections through a dedicated form. The introduction of the Refund option has indeed garnered some positive publicity for the Genius team. Some users believe that in the crypto industry, a project team's willingness to listen to feedback and update rules within 24 hours demonstrates a commitment to long-term sustainability, at least providing a clear exit strategy for users who no longer wish to hold the tokens. However, critics point out that the so-called refund only covers the 5 basis points charged by the platform itself, not the full transaction fee, which is woefully inadequate for a large number of users engaging in fraudulent trading. Some users believe that "long-termism is practically written into the code," and this entire operation—from raising expectations to changing the rules at the last minute—is just another scam; no matter how the refund option remedies the situation, the time and transaction costs already invested cannot be fully recovered. Even on Polymarket, some users have begun betting that Genius's FDV after launch will be less than $200 million, reflecting the market's loss of confidence in this TGE operation. The rules are flawed; long-term holders cannot be cultivated through points incentives alone. Leng, a crypto KOL specializing in project research and analysis, criticized Genius, stating that this approach is no different from fraud: "They could choose to give only 2% initially instead of 7% and then burn 70% after receiving 30%." How can users support this? The project team hopes to attract early users to contribute liquidity and trading volume through the points system, but during the token distribution phase, they want to prevent these users from immediately selling. There is a fundamental contradiction between these two goals: many users' core motivation is to quickly realize their rewards, while the long-term holders the project team envisions cannot be cultivated through points incentives alone. When an airdrop's claiming rules require a high-percentage burning mechanism to filter users, it likely indicates a fundamental mismatch in the mechanism's design. The user group it attracts and the user group it ultimately aims to retain are not the same people from the start.

data.title: Is X Planning A Crypto Move Inside X Money? Product Chief’s Comment Triggers Speculation On Future Digital Asset Integration
data.description:
Is X Preparing to Rescue the Crypto Market

Speculation is reaching a fever pitch across the digital asset space following a brief social media post from Nikita Bier, the head of product at X.

While the crypto market has struggled to find its footing throughout 2026, Bier’s recent comments suggest that the platform formerly known as Twitter might be preparing a major intervention.

On 14 April 2026, Bier posted a short message that immediately went viral, stating,

“Crypto has had a rough year. Maybe we should launch something to fix it.”

The remark, though vague, has been interpreted by many as a signal that X is finally ready to merge its massive social reach with native blockchain infrastructure.

Crypto has had a rough year. Maybe we should launch something to fix it.

— Nikita Bier (@nikitabier) April 14, 2026

What Will X Money Look Like At Launch

The timing of Bier’s comment aligns closely with the anticipated rollout of X Money, a financial services suite championed by Elon Musk.

Musk confirmed last month that the service is scheduled to go live in April 2026 across more than 40 U.S. states.

𝕏 Money early public access will launch next month

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) March 10, 2026

The initial stack is substantial, featuring peer-to-peer transfers, bank deposits, and a Visa-backed debit card offering cashback rewards.

While the public documentation describes a fiat-heavy product, the platform has notably avoided ruling out the use of blockchain rails.

Some analysts believe X might use crypto infrastructure behind the scenes to handle instant settlements and offer the high yields Musk has teased.

Will Digital Assets Be Integrated Into The Everything App

While the initial version of X Money appears focused on traditional banking, the technical DNA of the product team suggests a deeper crypto connection.

X recently recruited Benji Taylor, the former chief product officer at Aave and head of design at Base, to help lead the project.

Bier noted that he had tracked Taylor’s work for years and pushed for his hiring.

Ladies and gentlemen, I'd like to welcome @benjitaylor to 𝕏, our new design lead.

I met Benji six years ago when I invested in his app: it was one of the most well-designed products I'd encountered. I knew right away he was on track to become one of the best designers in the… https://t.co/TFTZGCIm29

— Nikita Bier (@nikitabier) March 25, 2026

Furthermore, beta testers have reported seeing features like "smart cashtags," which could allow users to view real-time charts and trade assets directly from their timeline.

Whether this includes dogecoin or bitcoin at launch remains a subject of intense debate among investors.

Can X Solve The Industry Bot Problem

Beyond new features, Bier has been vocal about the technical hurdles facing the industry, particularly the prevalence of automated manipulation.

He recently claimed that "80% of crypto is just bots," and later remarked,

“I think when we removed the crypto bots, there were only 2000 people rugging each other back and forth, forever.”

I think when we removed the crypto bots, there were only 2000 people rugging each other back and forth, forever

— Nikita Bier (@nikitabier) April 13, 2026

To combat this, X has introduced automated systems to detect account compromises and verify ownership when large accounts suddenly promote unknown tokens.

This focus on security suggests that any financial product launched by X will prioritise a clean, verified environment to protect users from the phishing scams that have plagued the sector.

How Will 6% Yields Change The Payment Landscape

One of the most competitive aspects of the upcoming launch is the potential for interest-bearing accounts.

Reports from the closed beta suggest X Money could offer up to 6% annual yield on stored balances, a figure that rivals many stablecoin-based savings products.

🚨BREAKING: X MONEY Beta REVEALS 6% APY — 600 MILLION USERS Could Soon Earn Yield Inside the App 🤯🔥

Early beta testers confirm that @XMoney is offering a straight 6% ANNUAL YIELD on cash balances held inside the app. 👀

That’s HIGHER than most U.S. high-yield savings accounts… https://t.co/Xi5EF6MBp5pic.twitter.com/nyhH6yNXdO

— Diana (@InvestWithD) March 2, 2026

By providing FDIC-insured deposits alongside high returns, X is positioning itself to compete not just with other social media platforms, but with traditional banks and crypto exchanges alike.

If the platform successfully bridges the gap between social engagement and financial utility, it could provide the mainstream momentum that the crypto market has lacked throughout a difficult 2026.

data.title: Robinhood's Transformation Path in Wealth Management
data.description:
Author: @BlazingKevin_, Blockbooster Researcher

Robinhood ushered in a turning point in its business model in 2025. By developing its wealth management business, including launching IRA retirement accounts with disruptive rates, introducing high-yield cash products, and offering a full range of Robinhood Banking services, Robinhood successfully guided its younger user base away from high-frequency options and cryptocurrency trading towards long-term savings and investment.

Robinhood's financial data in 2025 fully validated the success of this strategy: full-year net revenue reached a record $4.5 billion, a year-over-year increase of 52%; full-year net profit reached $1.9 billion, a year-over-year increase of 35%; assets under custody (AUC) in retirement accounts reached $26.5 billion at the end of the fourth quarter, a surge of 102% year-over-year; total assets on the platform reached $324 billion, a year-over-year increase of 68%; and full-year net deposits reached $68 billion.

This article will analyze Robinhood's wealth management business development trajectory in 2025 using data, and explore its strategic advantages around five core dimensions: customer acquisition and asset transfer mechanisms, profit model evolution, ecosystem closed loop, brand reshaping, and operating cost structure. 1. Customer Acquisition and Asset Transfer Mechanisms Traditional wealth management typically relies on financial advisors for high-cost customer acquisition and relationship maintenance. Robinhood, however, adopts a highly internet-based subsidy and incentive mechanism to break down barriers to asset transfer, thereby achieving rapid growth in asset management scale.

1.1 Matching Bonus: The Economics of "Paying to Buy AUM"

Traditional retirement account providers (such as Fidelity and Charles Schwab) typically rely on brand reputation and investment advisory services to attract customers, while Robinhood has adopted a more direct and aggressive strategy: cash matching bonuses. Using real cash rewards to reduce the psychological cost of user migration.

For Robinhood Gold members, the platform offers up to 3% IRA deposit matching (1% for non-members).

For Robinhood Gold members, the platform offers up to 3% IRA deposit matching (1% for non-members).

Based on the 2025 IRS contribution cap of $7,000 for those under 50, users could receive up to $210 in free matching funds annually. More aggressively, Robinhood also offers a matching bonus of up to 3% for 401(k) or IRA assets rolled over from other brokerages. This means a user transferring $100,000 in 401(k) assets to a Robinhood IRA could immediately receive a $3,000 cash bonus. Is this economically worthwhile? We can break it down from the perspective of Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) and Customer Lifetime Value (LTV). By the end of 2025, customers had accumulated over $500 million in matching funds for retirement account transfers and contributions. Robinhood considers this expenditure as a customer acquisition cost. Because retirement accounts have extremely high stickiness (typically held for decades), this asset not only generates long-term net interest income (NIM) and potential investment advisory fees, but also locks users into becoming Gold members (annual fee of $50). Compared to traditional brokerages' customer acquisition costs of hundreds of dollars and high churn rates, Robinhood uses a 3% subsidy to acquire decades of highly sticky assets, with its LTV far exceeding CAC. 1.2 Frictionless Account Transfer Experience: Technological Democracy Subsidies alone are not enough; if the transfer process is cumbersome, users will still be deterred. Robinhood uses technology to significantly lower the barrier for users to transfer from traditional brokerages. By integrating Automated Account Transfer Services (ACATS), Robinhood enables seamless asset transfers across brokerages. Users simply enter their original brokerage account information within the app; Robinhood's clearing system automatically transfers assets in the background without requiring manual liquidation of existing assets. Robinhood also reimburses transfer fees charged by some brokerages (typically $75) under certain conditions. This "one-click migration" experience completely breaks down the asset transfer barriers traditionally built by institutions through cumbersome processes. 2.
